Hello, and welcome to Storyworthy. My name is Matthew Dix. Congratulations. I don't know what's brought you here today to take this course. I don't know whether you discovered that storytelling was important all on your own, or you have a friend or a partner who has decided that becoming a better storyteller might be a good idea for you. Maybe you have a fellow business associate who has taken this course and has told you it's worthy. Whatever the reason you have come to storytelling today, you've made a great choice. Storytelling has changed my life. It has changed the lives of thousands of people who I have worked with over the years, and it's going to change your life too. I know that sounds like a lot of hubris. I know it sounds a little crazy. I know it might sound like nonsense, but it's none of those things. Truly, storytelling, when you become a better storyteller, it's going to change your life. Great storytellers are able to connect with other human beings. They're able to convince people to do things that they might not ordinarily do. They can get people to move in directions that they were not moving in previously. You can get closer to people. You can earn their trust. Storytelling is a superpower. It is something that few people do well, and when you do it well, it's going to make a huge difference in your life. Over the years I've worked with an enormous number of clients, including some of these there. Universities, corporations, priests and ministers and rabbis, bar associations, lots and lots of people have come to me and come to understand that storytelling can help their lives, change their careers, help them make their dreams come true. But I like this less better. This is the list of the unexpected folks who have come to me over the years. People like Santa Claus's, mall Santas who came to understand that if they could tell a great 30 second story to the kid who is sitting in their lap, then that kid is more likely to smile, get off their lap and get another kid onto their lap and therefore make more money. Or the grandparents who come to me and honestly tell me, I'm going to die before my grandchildren actually listen to my story, please help me tell a better story. Or storytelling for dating, which is very popular nowadays. These are people who understand that they have passed the I have good hygiene and I have a job test in order to get that first date. But whatever they say on that first date, it is not getting them a second date. It turns out that storytelling can be very helpful in this regard. I've worked with Olympic athletes. I've worked with people who have summited Mount Everest and want to tell a better story about summiting Mount Everest. I've had spouses send their spouse to me because frankly the stories that they come home with every day are not very entertaining and they want their spouse to be more enjoyable. For whatever reason you have come to this course, one of the great things about it is you're going to discover that even though you will meet your goals, you will find new goals that storytelling will help you with as well. You may be looking to gain investment in your business or sell more widgets. Maybe you want to improve your marketing plan or deliver better talks to your employees. What you'll discover is storytelling has a role in your everyday life and every part of your life. That as long as you're communicating with people, as long as you're trying to connect with other human beings, storytelling can help you. So congratulations, you've made a great choice being here. Now, I always think in situations like this, you should get to know the person who will be teaching you. I've taken courses like this before and halfway through I've wondered who is this person who's speaking to me and can I trust them? So I want to talk a little bit about how I landed here today, why you're listening to me and not someone else. I think it's important to know that I did not grow up in the lap of a great storyteller. I did not grow up in a culture of storytelling. I did not go off to some university that taught me to become a storyteller. None of these things are true. I grew up in a broken home. I grew up in a home where my brothers and sisters and parents and I, we were sort of private contractors. We operated our own separate businesses in the corners of the house and we occasionally came together to have a meal together and then retire to our separate corners. I didn't grow up with adults who were telling me stories and I didn't grow up with adults who wanted to hear my stories. I didn't go to school to become a storyteller. None of these things are true. And that means none of these things need to be true for you. You don't even need to have a great facility for the English language in order to tell a great story. As a competitive storyteller, I often go to New York and I compete against a woman named Denousha. For Denousha, English is her second or maybe third language. Her nouns and verbs don't always agree. My vocabulary is absolutely more expansive than hers is. And yet she kicks my butt routinely in storytelling competitions because she understands the important parts of storytelling. She understands that storytelling is really about decision making. It's about thinking about what you will say before you say it. And Denousha does a brilliant job. And you can too. So I came to storytelling back in 2009. My friends came to me. They were listening to a podcast produced by an organization called The Moth. The Moth is sort of the granddaddy of all storytelling organizations, an internationally known organization that produces storytelling shows around the world, including competitive storytelling competitions. My friends were listening to their podcasts and they said, you should become a storyteller. You would be a great storyteller, they told me, because you've lived the worst life of anyone we know, which is really not a nice thing to say about your friend. It's also not true. For the last 24 years, I've been teaching in an elementary school, a title one school, with kids who have suffered from far greater struggles than I ever have in my life. But it is true that I've lived one of those unusual lives, or at least one of those lives that are marked by unusual events. Twice in my life, I have stopped breathing and my heart has stopped beating, and paramedics have resuscitated me through CPR. I was arrested and tried for a crime I didn't commit. I was homeless for a period in my life. I was the victim of a horrific armed robbery that has left me with a lifetime of PTSD. And that's sort of the tip of the iceberg of the nonsense that has been my life. And so my friend said, go to New York, compete in some of those moth storytelling competitions. Tell the story about the time you were rescued from your bed by a firefighter as your house burned, another true story from my life. And I said, yes, I said, I'll go to New York, I'll tell those crazy stories, and I'll be a great storyteller. And so my wife and I went in July of 2011 to a story slam at the New Aureacan's Poets Cafe in New York City. At a moth story slam, what you do is you put your name into a bag and then 10 names over the course of the evening are drawn from that bag and those 10 people will tell stories. As soon as I put my name in the bag, I prayed to God that my name would not be chosen. I had agreed to go to this story slam because my friends convinced me that I would be great. But once I found myself actually in the venue, sitting there staring at a stage, I discovered I don't want to be a storyteller. I don't want to share my story to a group of disaffected hipsters with man buns and side eye who would judge me and actually assign a numerical rating to my performance. It was terrifying. Here's a picture that was taken that night in the New Aureacan's Poets Cafe. It was taken by Dan Kennedy, the host of the moth that night. He stood on stage and he took a picture of the audience. Look at that audience. Do you see how excited everyone is? I'm actually in the picture. Can you see me? Look to the left. Look to the left side of that picture. Here I'll zoom in. Do you see me? I'm the only person in the room that night who is upset about being there. Even my wife who is sitting just to the right of me and behind me, she's excited. She's excited because she doesn't have to tell a story. I am upset. I am genuinely annoyed and disappointed and angry that I have been tricked into telling a story at this moth story slam. And so I put my name in the hat and prayed that my name would not come out. And miraculously, nine names had been chosen over the course of the night and my name had not come out. And I was already sort of mentally in my car driving home when the host of the evening, Dan Kennedy, drew the 10th name out of that bag and it was my name. And at first I didn't move. I just sat very still and very quietly because it occurred to me that no one in this room knows me. So if I don't say anything, eventually that slip that said Matthew Dix would be tossed aside and another name would be drawn from the bag. But fortunately, my terrible wife was with me that night. She was sitting across the wobbly table from me. And so when she heard my name and she saw me not move, she kicked me under the table and she said, that's your name. And I said, I know. I don't want to tell my story anymore. And she said, get on the stage and tell your story. And so grudgingly I stood up and I made my way to that stage and I told the first story publicly of my life, a story about pole vaulting in high school and how I was rooting against my teammates so that I might be perceived as the best polevolter on the team. And miraculously, I won the slam that night. More importantly, I discovered something on that stage. I hated every moment of that night until I began speaking into the microphone. As soon as I began speaking to that audience, I discovered I had found something that I loved. I used to tell people I'm naturally good at storytelling. I stumbled upon something later in life. I went and tried it and right away I was good. And then once again, my wife one day in a workshop heard me say that and said, you're a fool. I really believe that you're just naturally good as a storyteller. And I said, yeah, well, I didn't go to school for it. I didn't grow up in the lap of a storyteller. Why would I be good at this? My wife pointed out to me that I've been a writer all of my life. I've published novels. I understand how story works. When I was a little kid, I used to watch movies. And after watching E.T. one night, I became so angry with a scene in the movie E.T. that I wrote a letter to Steven Spielberg and gave it to my mother. The letter essentially said, dear Steven, I can't stand how you always screw up your movies with one dumb thing. So if you would send me your movie before you put it in the theaters, I'll tell you what the one dumb thing is so you get to have perfect movies. Steven Spielberg never responded to my letter. And it occurs to me today that probably my mother never sent that letter. I suspect in 1982, without the internet, it was difficult to find an address for Steven Spielberg. I suspect she either threw the note away or tucked it away somewhere and forgot about it. But my wife's point is correct. I've sort of been obsessed with stories for a long time, both on the screen and on the page. I've also been a wedding DJ for more than 20 years, which means every Saturday night for a long, long time, I have been standing in front of large groups of people and speaking extemporaneously, learning how to use a microphone, learning how to adjust my voice, becoming comfortable in front of large groups of people. And it turns out that can be helpful in a storytelling competition. I'm also an elementary school teacher. Since 1999, I've been teaching second, third and fifth graders, which turns out to be the worst audience there ever was. Miserable little children who don't want to listen to me. And so I had to learn every trick to get those kids to listen to me, tricks that I use on the stage today. And so I became a storyteller accidentally. But over the course of the years that I have been a storyteller, I have been deconstructing the craft. I'm an elementary school teacher, so I am trained to be able to take large processes like long division and break them into small, repeatable parts. That is what I have done for storytelling. I have taken this large craft, this art that some people refer to it, and I've broken it down into parts that I can now teach you. Parts that you can practice and repeat. Practice that you can use in your everyday life. Parts that you can begin using to tell better stories and begin finding out that storytelling really is a superpower. That being able to go into the world and sit in a restaurant or play a round of golf with your friends or drive in a car with a group of people and be entertaining and connective and authentic and vulnerable to reach into hearts and minds and become memorable, all of those things will change your life. I don't know what goals you have. I don't know what's brought you to this series. I don't know why you want to become a better storyteller. All of it is sort of irrelevant to me because I know that you will quickly discover that whatever goal brought you here, it will soon be one of many. You will soon discover that for whatever reason you think storytelling will help you, there were a multitude of reasons why storytelling will help you. Before we move on to the next video, just a couple things I want you to keep in mind. First, it's normal to feel anxious, to lack confidence, to be nervous. We know that public speaking is the thing that frightens people the most. So if you're entering into this process feeling nervous in any way, it means you're a human being. Congratulations. It's going to be okay. You're no different than anyone else. I often compare storytelling to sort of the high dive. If you've ever gone off a high dive before, you can watch a thousand people go off a high dive, watch every single one of them hit the water, return to the surface and climb out of the pool unharmed. You could watch a thousand of them, but when it comes time for you to go off the high dive, it's still terrifying. It doesn't matter how many people go before you, that first jump off a high dive is a scary thing. Scary things, hard things, they're often the right thing. So congratulations, you're human. You're nervous and that's okay. You should also know that you don't need to have a crazy life like the one that I have lived. The thing that my friends got wrong and the thing that I got wrong was I thought that the best stories I would tell would be about those crazy moments of my life. Truthfully, I don't tell those stories very often and lots of them I have never told before. The story of being arrested and tried for a crime I did not commit is not a story I have ever told. Not because it won't be a great story, not because people won't enjoy it, but simply because it turns out that the small moments of our lives, small moments of meaning, absent arrests and trials and car chases and parachute jumps and crimes and misdemeanors, all of those small moments are some of the best stories that we can tell. Small moments are relatable moments. So if you're thinking, I've never died and come back to life, I've never been homeless, I've never been the victim of a violent crime, congratulations. You're a very lucky person. You don't need any of them to tell a great story. I promise you. Lastly, I want you to believe me when I tell you that by the time we're done, you will be a great storyteller. I promise you this. I have not met a person who I can't work with and turned into a great storyteller. Storytelling is not an art. It is a craft. It is a learned craft. And I have spent an enormous amount of time taking that craft and breaking it down so that I can teach you to do it. There's a quote that I love from a movie that I love. The quote is, what one man can do, another can do. And I think in storytelling, it applies perfectly. By the time you're done here, you'll be a great storyteller. All I need you to do is to believe in that and believe in me. So let's get started.
